title: Same Tree
description: Given the roots of two binary trees p and q, write a function to check if they are the same or not. Two binary trees are considered the same if they are structurally identical and the nodes have the same value.
hints: Use recursion to compare both trees simultaneously.
Both trees are the same if their roots have the same value and their left and right subtrees are the same.
Base cases: if both nodes are null, return true; if one is null and the other is not, return false.
spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth. A binary tree's maximum depth is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.
hints: Use recursion to traverse the tree.
The depth of a node is 1 + max(depth of left subtree, depth of right subtree).
Base case: an empty node has depth 0.
spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

title: Balanced Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree, determine if it is height-balanced. A height-balanced binary tree is a binary tree in which the depth of the two subtrees of every node never differs by more than one.
hints: Use postorder DFS to compute the height of each subtree.
At each node, check if the left and right subtree heights differ by more than 1.
Use a helper function that returns -1 if a subtree is unbalanced, otherwise returns its height.
spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

title: Path Sum
description: Given the root of a binary tree and an integer targetSum, return true if the tree has a root-to-leaf path such that adding up all the values along the path equals targetSum. A leaf is a node with no children.
hints: Use DFS to traverse from root to leaf.
Subtract the current node's value from the target sum as you traverse.
When you reach a leaf node, check if the remaining sum equals the leaf's value.
spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

title: Invert Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree, and return its root. Inverting means swapping the left and right children of every node.
hints: Use a recursive DFS approach.
For each node, swap its left and right children.
Then recursively invert the left and right subtrees.
spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

title: Diameter of Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the length of the diameter of the tree. The diameter of a binary tree is the length of the longest path between any two nodes in a tree. This path may or may not pass through the root. The length of a path between two nodes is represented by the number of edges between them.
hints: The diameter through any node is the sum of the heights of its left and right subtrees.
Use DFS to compute the height of each node while updating the diameter.
The maximum diameter found during traversal is the answer.
spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST). A valid BST is defined as follows: the left subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys less than the node's key, the right subtree contains only nodes with keys greater than the node's key, and both left and right subtrees must also be binary search trees.
hints: Use DFS with a valid range for each node.
For the root, the range is (-infinity, +infinity).
When going left, update the upper bound to the current node's value.
When going right, update the lower bound to the current node's value.
spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

title: Path Sum II
description: Given the root of a binary tree and an integer targetSum, return all root-to-leaf paths where the sum of the node values in the path equals targetSum. Each path should be returned as a list of the node's values, in order from root to leaf.
hints: Use DFS with backtracking.
Maintain a current path list and add/remove nodes as you traverse.
When you reach a leaf with the correct sum, add a copy of the current path to the result.
timeComplexity: O(n^2) in the worst case due to path copying
spaceComplexity: O(n) for the recursion stack and path storage

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree, find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two given nodes in the tree. According to the definition of LCA on Wikipedia: the lowest common ancestor is defined between two nodes p and q as the lowest node in T that has both p and q as descendants (where we allow a node to be a descendant of itself).
hints: Use postorder DFS traversal.
If the current node is null or equal to p or q, return the current node.
Recursively search left and right subtrees.
If both left and right return non-null, the current node is the LCA.
If only one side returns non-null, propagate that result up.
spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
description: Design an algorithm to serialize and deserialize a binary tree. There is no restriction on how your serialization/deserialization algorithm should work. You just need to ensure that a binary tree can be serialized to a string and this string can be deserialized to the original tree structure.
hints: Use preorder DFS to serialize the tree.
Use a special marker (like 'null' or '#') to represent null nodes.
For deserialization, read values one by one and reconstruct the tree using the same preorder approach.
Use an iterator or index to keep track of the current position during deserialization.
timeComplexity: O(n) for both serialize and deserialize
spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing the serialized string and recursion stack
